coredata.dc:title: Hydrothermal synthesis and photoluminescent mechanistic investigation of highly fluorescent nitrogen doped carbon dots from amino acids
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Juan
Chen, Weilong
Liu, Xueping
Zhang, Yao
Bai, Yan
coredata.dc:description: A green and facile method was developed to synthesize nitrogen doped carbon dots (NCDs) with blue-luminescence by hydrothermal treatment of different amino acids (aspartic acid, glutamic, proline, threonine, valine, phenylalanine, arginine and serine). The NCDs were characterized by various methods and the highest quantum yield reached 41.3%. The formation and surface passivation of NCDs were accomplished simultaneously via dehydration, polymerization, carbonization, and passivation process. The passivation degree of NCDs was controlled by the hydrothermal time. NCDs had good aqueous dispersibility, high stability, and preeminent photoluminescent properties. The emission of NCDs was highly dependent on excitation energy, as the excitation wavelength increased from 300 to 470nm, the emission peak position shifted from blue to green region with the intensity decreased. These features resulted from the fact that the surface passivation could form the additional interband and we explained the relationship between luminescent properties and energy band in detail.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Amino acids
Nitrogen doped carbon dots
Hydrothermal
Photoluminescent mechanism

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and functionalization of NCDs from amino acids
Characterization of NCDs formed from amino acids
Measurement for quantum yield and elemental analysis
Formation mechanism of NCDs from amino acids
Photoluminescent properties of NCDs formed from amino acids

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of the proposed energy band diagram model based on NCDs-Asp to explain the possible PL emission mechanism of NCDs. The emission peak intensity of NCDs-Asp was normalized in the PL spectra.

AFM images of NCDs-Asp (a) and NCDs-Glu (c) on mica substrate; Height profiles of NCDs-Asp (b) and NCDs-Glu (d) corresponding to the AFM image. TEM images of NCDs-Asp (e) and NCDs-Glu (g) and size distribution histograms of NCDs-Asp (f) and NCDs-Glu (h).

XRD patterns of amino acids (Asp, Glu, Thr) and corresponding NCDs in different hydrothermal time. (a) Asp and NCDs-Asp synthesized by hydrothermal treatment 5, 10 and 18h; (b) Glu and NCDs-Glu synthesized by hydrothermal treatment 10 and 18h; (c) Thr and NCDs-Thr synthesized by hydrothermal treatment 18h. Vertical lines show the positions of graphite (PDF 08-0415).
FTIR spectra of amino acids and corresponding NCDs in different hydrothermal time. (a) Asp and NCDs-Asp; (b) Glu and NCDs-Glu; (c) Thr and NCDs-Thr.
Schematic formation of NCDs-Asp through dehydration, polymerization, carbonization and passivation.

PL spectra (a) and corresponding color coordinate (b) of the NCDs-Asp at different excitation wavelengths and effects of the NaCl concentration and pH on the PL intensity of NCDs-Asp (c). Inset in (a) is the photograph of the NCDs-Asp under daylight (left) and 365nm UV light (right).
UV–vis absorption and PL emission spectra of NCDs synthesized by different amino acids: Glu (a), Val (b), Phe (c), Arg (d), Pro (e), Thr (f), Cys (g), Ser (h).
Elemental Analysis and PL QYs of NCDs (hydrothermal time 18h).

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The values=Average±Standard deviation; Experiment number n=3.

head.ce:title: Hydrothermal synthesis and photoluminescent mechanistic investigation of highly fluorescent nitrogen doped carbon dots from amino acids

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Jinan University, Guangzhou 510632, People’s Republic of China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The PL of NCDs was contributed by two transition processes. Process 1 was band to band transition under higher excitation energy. Process 2 was interstate emission and excitation-dependent under the lower excitation energy.

A green and facile method was developed to synthesize nitrogen doped carbon dots (NCDs) with blue-luminescence by hydrothermal treatment of different amino acids (aspartic acid, glutamic, proline, threonine, valine, phenylalanine, arginine and serine). The NCDs were characterized by various methods and the highest quantum yield reached 41.3%. The formation and surface passivation of NCDs were accomplished simultaneously via dehydration, polymerization, carbonization, and passivation process. The passivation degree of NCDs was controlled by the hydrothermal time. NCDs had good aqueous dispersibility, high stability, and preeminent photoluminescent properties. The emission of NCDs was highly dependent on excitation energy, as the excitation wavelength increased from 300 to 470nm, the emission peak position shifted from blue to green region with the intensity decreased. These features resulted from the fact that the surface passivation could form the additional interband and we explained the relationship between luminescent properties and energy band in detail.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) have received considerable attention because of their attractive photoelectric properties and potential applications in the fields of bioimaging [1,2], detecting [3–5], diagnosing [6], and catalysis [7] etc. Compared to organic dyes and semiconductor quantum dots, CDs offer the advantages of low toxicity, excellent biocompatibility, and good stability for biological application [8]. Thus, they have been proposed to be promising substitutes of fluorescent semiconductor quantum dots in the future.

CDs are generally consisted of three common elements: carbon, hydrogen and oxygen [9]. Some protein-rich biomass materials [3], and carbohydrates [10] usually used as the precursors for the synthesis of CDs. Moreover, hetero atoms (N and S) doped in CDs, especially N could effectively improve the fluorescence emission and introduce some new surface states [11,12]. Zhang et al. reported the CDs with different N contents synthesized by using CCl4 and NaNH2 as starting materials, showed high quantum yields (QY) [13].

Amino acids, biologically important organic compounds possessing both amino and carboxyl groups, could act as perfect molecular precursor for synthesizing N doped carbon dots (NCDs). Hsu et al. reported a simple hydrothermal approach for the preparation of CDs from glycine and the quantum yields reached up to 30.6% [14]. Meanwhile, Jiang et al. likewise used amino acids (histidine and alanine) as the source for producing NCDs by microwave irradiation in the presence of acid or alkali [15]. Furthermore, Yang’ group reported a hydrothermal approach for the preparation of NCDs from l-tryptophan and l-serine in the weak alkaline water, whose highest QY reached 61.12% in a low pH [16] and their latest study about SNCDs showed that the chemical structure and photoluminescence (PL) were heavily influenced by the synthesis pH [17]. Although great efforts have been made to synthesize NCDs from N containing organic compounds, the systematic on using different kinds of amino acids to synthesis NCDs were still limited. Therefore, developing a simple, convenient and friendly route for for synthetizing of NCDs from different amino acids is a worthy project.

Herein, we developed a one-step hydrothermal method for synthetizing highly fluorescent N doped carbon dots from amino acids. The different types of amino acids were used as carbon sources as well as passivating agents for synthetizing NCDs without any other additives, and a family of NCDs obtained from the corresponding amino acids. Our results revealed that the surface of NCDs was passivated by amounts of carboxyl, hydroxyl and amino groups at their surface, and the NCDs exhibited good water soluble and stability as well as high QY. The mechanism behind the green and blue PL was also discussed.

Aspartic acid (C4H7NO4, Asp), glutamic (C5H9NO4, Glu), proline (C5H9NO2, Pro), threonine (C4H9NO3, Thr), valine (C5H11NO2, Val), phenylalanine (C9H11NO2, Phe), arginine (C6H11N4O2, Arg) and serine (C3H7NO3, Ser) were from Shanghai Bio Science & Technology Co. Ltd. (China). Sodium chloride, sodium hydroxide and hydrochloric acid were bought from Guangzhou Chemical Reagent Factory (China). All of the chemicals used in this work were analytical grade and used as received without further purification. Water was purified using a Milli-Q-system (Millipore, Bedford, MA, USA).

Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were obtained from JEM-2100F transmission electron microscope (JEOL Ltd., Japan) at an acceleration voltage of 200.00kV. Atomic force microscope (AFM) images were taken in Bioscope CatylystNanoscope-V atomic force microscope (Bruker, USA). X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained from a MSAL-XD2 X-ray diffraction (Beijing, China). The Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR) were measured by a Nicolet 6700 spectrometer (Bruker, USA). Fluorescence measurements were performed on a 970 CRT fluorescence spectrophotometer (Shanghai, China). UV–visible absorption spectra were recorded by a Cary 5000 UV–visible spectrophotometer (Varian, USA). Element analysis were measured by a EA2400 II CHNS/O Elemental Analyzer (PE, USA). The experimental characterizations were performed at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and functionalization of NCDs from amino acids
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescent NCDs were synthetized by one-step hydrothermal method. In a typical synthesis, 0.1331g Asp was dissolved into 20mL water and stirred vigorously for 20min. Then transferred the clear solution into 50mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 180°C for a fixed time. After cooling to room temperature, the centrifugation (11,000rpm, 30min) was followed and the supernatant containing fluorescent NCDs-Asp was collected. Finally, the products were collected by dialysis against deionized water through a dialysis membrane (500 MWCO) for 24h. The powder of NCDs-Asp was obtained by lyophilisation. Other fluorescent NCDs, such as NCDs-Val, NCDs-Phe, NCDs-Arg, NCDs-Pro, NCDs-Thr, NCDs-Ser, NCDs-Glu, were similarly synthesized.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of NCDs formed from amino acids
ce:section.ce:para: The formation of NCDs was confirmed by AFM and TEM measurements. AFM images of NCDs-Asp and NCDs-Glu are shown in Fig. 1 . The topographic heights NCDs-Asp were mostly less than 1.5nm, with an average height of about 1.0nm (Fig. 1a and b), while the average heights of NCDs-Glu was 2.0nm with a distribution in the range of 1.0–2.5nm (Fig. 1c and d). The results suggested that NCDs-Asp were single-layered, similar to those observed in functionalized graphene CDs with only one layer, while NCDs-Glu were multi-layered graphene CDs (2–3 layers) [18–20]. TEM images of NCDs-Asp and NCDs-Glu are shown in Fig. 1(e and g), the NCDs were uniform and monodisperse nanoparticles with near spherical shape and an interlayer spacing of about 0.21nm (NCDs-Asp, insert in Fig. 1e) and 0.23nm (NCDs-Glu, insert in Fig. 1g) which was close to the (102) graphite peak, indicating the formation of the graphite structures [21]. Meanwhile, a statistical histogram of NCDs size distributions was in a narrow range, varying from 2.8–6.3nm (NCDs-Asp, Fig. 1f) and 3.0–5.0nm (NCDs-Glu, Fig. 1h).

Furthermore, the XRD and FTIR analysis of products in different preparation stages was carried out. As shown in Fig. 2 , a series of significant changes in XRD patterns of products were observed at different hydrothermal time. For example, in Fig. 2a, some peaks in the XRD pattern of Asp disappeared gradually with the hydrothermal time increasing, leaving only one broad and weakened peak at around 25° for 18h, which matched up with (002) facet of the graphite-like structure (PDF 08-0415) [15,22]. The result suggested that Asp gradually transformed into the NCDs-Asp which had the thin structure and was also consistent with the AFM result. Compared with NCDs-Asp, peaks of amino acids were still observed in the XRD patterns of some NCDs for hydrothermal treatment 18h, such as NCDs-Glu (Fig. 2b), NCDs-Thr (Fig. 2c). These results showed that the time played a pivotal role in the hydrothermal synthesis of NCDs at 180°C.

Chemical and structural information about the products was also obtained by the FTIR. The spectra of amino acids and their hydrothermal products in different hydrothermal times are presented in Fig. 3 and there follows a detailed description of the hydrothermal process of Asp. As shown in Fig. 3a, when the Asp was hydrothermal treated within 5h, the absorption bands of the products displayed several characteristics of Asp, such as the overlapping absorption band of OH and NH stretching vibration at 3000cm−1 and CH asymmetrical and symmetrical stretching vibration of CH2 at 2950 and 2850cm−1, indicating that the product contain large amounts of incompletely reacted Asp. As hydrothermal time prolonged, some absorption bands of Asp disappeared gradually and the FTIR spectra of products exhibited obvious characteristics of condensation reaction and carbonization. For example, the absorption bands around 1580cm−1 and 1700cm−1 were bending vibration of amide II (in-plane NH bending) and amide I (CO stretching vibration) and the absorption band around 1400cm−1 assigned to amide III (CN in amide bonds), which corresponding to the FTIR of GQDs [23]. In addition, a broad band around 3100–3600cm−1 caused by OH stretching vibration overlapped with NH stretching vibration of the amide bond, which was wider than Asp and a blue shift due to the formation of the amide group. Finally, after 30h, the main absorption bands were not changed except in the area of 400–1000cm−1, indicating that Asp has been carbonized completely in this condition [24]. These results approved that polycondensation reaction happened between carboxyl and amino groups at first, then the hydrothermal products enriched. Other NCDs made from amino acids also showed the similar results, such as NCDs-Glu and NCDs-Thr (Fig. 3b and c).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement for quantum yield and elemental analysis
ce:section.ce:para: Although CDs have become attractive materials recently, their application was limited by low QY (often less than 10%) [22,25]. NCDs commonly have more perfect QY than CDs without N doping [3,26]. The compositions and QYs of the NCDs synthesized by the hydrothermal treatment 18h at 180°C are shown in Table 1 . The N content of NCDs was more than 10% and the ratio of N/C was higher, notably, NCDs-Asp displayed the QY of 41.3% which was close to the value reported by Li et al. [27]. Meanwhile, a series of optical image of NCDs solution under 365nm UV lamp (Fig. S1, Supporting information) also showed the bright blue luminescence which was strong enough to be easily seen with the naked eyes. In addition, the formation and surface properties of NCDs were probably responsible for the QY, so the synthesis condition, especially hydrothermal time, played an important role in this work. The hydrothermal time for 18h could ensure to synthesize NCDs with blue-luminescence from amino acids, but not the best synthesis condition for all kinds of NCDs. For example, compared with NCDs-Asp, luminescence intensities of NCDs-Glu and NCDs-Thr synthesized by hydrothermal treatment 18h were not the strongest (Fig. S1), especially, NCDs-Thr presented a minor QYs though its N content and its ratio of N/C were higher than three other NCDs. The reason may be that most of the N elements were not doped into carbon framework in a hydrothermal treatment 18h and made little contribution to improve the emission [11]. Meanwhile, the results of XRD and FTIR also showed that the degree of surface passivation for the three NCDs was different (see Figs. 2 and 3). Therefore, the QYs in Table 1 were not necessarily the highest. As described above, NCDs synthesized from different amino acids showed different QYs and the amino acid was considered to be an excellent material for NCDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Formation mechanism of NCDs from amino acids
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs commonly have a carbon core and an amorphous shell with oxygen-containing groups [11]. For the synthesizing of NCDs, amino acids were carbon and nitrogen source, as well as providing surface passivating groups such as NH2, COOH and OH, thereby generating a carbon core bond with nitrogen [13] and an amorphous shell with amino groups and oxygen-containing groups. According to the proposed formation mechanism of CDs by Li’s group [28], the NCDs synthesized from amino acids proceed via four stages, including dehydration, polymerization, carbonization and surface passivation as depicted in Fig. 4 . Take NCDs-Asp for an example, the formation process of NCDs for the hydrothermal treatment at 180°C is described as follows. In the primary stage of hydrothermal treatment, large-sized polymers from the dehydration of amino acids were formed and polycondensation reaction between carboxyl and amino groups has been approved by FTIR spectra above. As shown in UV–vis spectra (Fig. S2, see Supporting information), in a short hydrothermal time like 3h, an absorption band appeared in the 250–300nm range and then it shifted to the long wavelength upon further increasing hydrothermal time, revealing the polymerization of Asp [14,29] and formation of NCDs-Asp. In addition, after 6h treatment, the polymers began to carbonize (Fig. S3a, see Supporting information). Subsequently, large-sized particles began to shrink by continuous carbonization process, leading to a short single burst of nucleation (Fig. S3b and c). With the prolongation of hydrothermal time, very uniform and monodisperse NCDs-Asp were obtained (Fig. S3d). Similar results have been reported by Ding’s group [30]. In the process of hydrothermal treatment, N elements were doped into carbon framework to form the NCDs core with graphene-like carbon rings structures and the N-doping amount increased with prolonged hydrothermal reaction time [13]. In addition, the core of NCDs peripherally modified with amino groups and oxygen-containing groups to form the shell of surface passivation. As amino groups and oxygen-containing groups were unstable and tended to leave the surface after longer hydrothermal reaction time, the surface passivation was time-varying process. As shown in Fig. S1, the PL intensities of NCDs also changed with time and stronger fluorescence intensities were observed in the NCDs synthesized at 15–20h, indicating that appropriate surface passivation could result in a significant increase in PL intense [27]. The NCDs had more surface traps at the beginning of the formation, then gradually tended to be perfected through modification of amino groups and oxygen-containing groups, so the PL of NCDs strengthen gradually. After a certain time, surface traps increased again as the modifying groups left the surface and the PL of NCDs weakened gradually, which indicated that NCDs were partially passivated or almost non-passivated when hydrothermally treated overtime. The dynamic change process in the formation and surface passivation of NCDs were illustrated in the XRD and FTIR. In addition, compared to the partly-passivated or almost non-passivated ones, the emission peaks of fully-passivated NCDs lightly shifted to the short wavelength direction. This phenomenon resulted from OH groups bounding on the surface of NCDs [13], because most of the COOH on the surface turned into OH in hydrothermal reaction. These results demonstrated that both the carbonization including N-doping and surface passivation were controlled by the treatment time at a constant hydrothermal temperature of 180°C. Therefore, according to the experimental results, a comprehensive consideration of the N-doping amount and surface passivation degree, we chose 18h as the hydrothermal reaction time, unless otherwise specified.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photoluminescent properties of NCDs formed from amino acids
ce:section.ce:para: Like most luminescent CDs, the NCDs displayed the excitation wavelength dependent fluorescence behavior. For example, when the excitation wavelength was changed from 300 to 470nm, the corresponding PL emission peaks of NCDs-Asp (hydrothermal time 15h) shifted from 397 to 513nm (Fig. 5 a). Meanwhile, the intensity of emission peaks became weak gradually with emission color range from blue to green in the corresponding Commission International d’Eclairage coordinates (CIE) of the samples (Fig. 5b) and the strongest emission peak at 405nm (λex =340nm) in accordance with Table S2 (see Supporting information). Moreover, the PL intensity of NCDs slightly change with NaCl concentrations (0.1–0.5M) and changed with pH varying from 2 to 12 as shown in Fig. 5c. Other NCDs also had the similar PL properties (Fig. 6 ) and the strongest emission peaks of the NCDs occurred at 400–470nm with a Stokes shift of 64–80nm.

Herein, we investigated the PL mechanism of NCDs. First of all, we explored the outer electronic transition from a bonding orbital to antibonding orbital, such as π-π* and n-π* transition in NCDs by investigating the characteristics of UV–vis spectra. As shown in Fig. 6, the UV–vis spectra of NCDs exhibited wide electronic band gap in the UV region (230–350nm) with a tail extending into the visible range and with an absorption peak at around 270nm, which was typical of that generally reported for other CDs [27,31]. More importantly, the absorption peak at around 270nm was attributed to π-π* transition of aromatic structure CC bonds for carbon core [31], while the absorption band at 300–320 was attributed to n-π* transition of CO bonds or other connected group [32]. Since amino and hydroxy groups, as surface passivation groups, were electron donating and could enhance the conjugation degree of NCDs, the absorption peak of π-π* transition was red-shift. The UV–vis spectra indicated that the larger energy band of NCDs was due to electronic states with well-defined conjugate aromatic structure and surface passivation.

Subsequently, in order to better understand the PL mechanism of NCDs formed from amino acids, we further explored the energy transformation in PL process. The more information about the excitation energy (E x), emission energy (E m) and the optical band gaps (E g) of NCDs are showed in Table S1 (Supporting information). As shown above, from maximum excitation wavelength, the lowest direct absorption edge of NCDs was found to be 3.10–3.76eV (average 3.47eV). Further, from the broad absorption of NCDs we estimated the apparent optical band gap energy. The direct optical band gap energy of NCDs was found to be around 3.77–5.17eV (average 4.50eV). In fact, the optical band gap was corresponded to the energy difference between the highest occupied molecular orbital and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (HOMO–LUMO gap, that is E g.). It is notable that the value of the optical band gap (4.50eV) of NCDs was greater than its excitation energy (3.47eV, Table S1), indicating that there was an additional interband in between HOMO and LUMO. Kumar et al. suggested that an additional interband around 3.28eV could be formed within the energy gap of amino-functionalized grapheme quantum dot by p orbital hybridization in between CN atoms at the edge sites [33]. Since the excitation energy of the strongest emission intensity of NCDs was closed to the value of 3.28eV, we inferred that a new interstate energy level at around 3.10–3.76eV (the excitation energy, Table S1) was formed in between the HOMO and LUMO states of NCDs like functionalized graphene quantum dots. Since NCDs had much larger energy band (4.50eV) and an additional interband (3.28eV) within the energy gap, the NCDs possessed the PL mainly in range of blue to green section [31,33]. In order to explain this result, take NCDs-Asp for an example, the possible PL emission mechanism of NCDs was schematically shown in Scheme 1 . In the case, the PL was contributed by two different transition processes, namely, transition process 1 and 2. For transition process 1, the PL was band to band transition, that is, the transitions from the HOMO to LUMO by higher excitation energy (λex <λex(max)), E ex was closed to HOMO–LUMO gap), which was the blue emission and not excitation-dependent PL behavior. For transition process 2, the PL was interstate or defect induced emission, in which the transition from the HOMO to the additional interband occurred under the lower excitation energy (λex ≥λex(max)) and the PL was strong excitation-dependent. The surface passivation of NCDs could form the additional interband which reduced the excitation energy of the system compared with that in the transition from band to band, and which was also related to the excitation-dependent PL behavior.

We have successfully developed a green and facile approach to synthesize N doped carbon dots by one step hydrothermal treatment of different amino acids. The formation and surface passivation of NCDs were accomplished simultaneously. The NCDs possessed single or few layers graphene-like carbon rings structures and surface passivation by amino and oxygen-containing groups and showed excellent aqueous dispersibility, and considerable stability. Furthermore, the PL emission of NCDs exhibited higher QYs (13.3–41.3%). Significantly, the research showed a strong correlation between the surface passivation and the PL feature of NCDs. The surface passivation could form the additional interband which played a pivotal role in reducing the excitation energy of NCDs, leading to the PL emission from the blue to green region in excitation wavelength dependence. A family of NCDs based on amino acids was attractive because of its simplicity and favorable optical properties.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21075053).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.materresbull.2017.01.013.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An aqueous route to multicolor photoluminescent carbon dots using silica spheres as carriers
Green synthesis of stable and biocompatible fluorescent carbon dots from peanut shells for multicolor living cell imaging
Nitrogen-doped photoluminescent carbon nanospheres: green, simple synthesis via hair and application as a sensor for Hg2+ ions
Ultra-sensitive and selective Hg2+ detection based on fluorescent carbon dots
Facilely synthesized N-doped carbon quantum dots with high fluorescent yield for sensing Fe3+
Distinguish cancer cells based on targeting turn-on fluorescence imaging by folate functionalized green emitting carbon dots
Upconversion and downconversion fluorescent graphene quantum dots: ultrasonic preparation and photocatalysis
Dual functional carbon dots derived from cornflour via a simple one-pot hydrothermal route
Microwave synthesis of fluorescent carbon nanoparticles with electrochemiluminescence properties
Nitrogen-doped carbon dots: a facile and general preparation method, photoluminescence investigation, and imaging applications
Carbon-based dots Co-doped with nitrogen and sulfur for high quantum yield and excitation-independent emission
One-pot synthesis of N-doped carbon dots with tunable luminescence properties
Synthesis of high-quality carbon nanodots from hydrophilic compounds: role of functional groups
Amino acids as the source for producing carbon nanodots: microwave assisted one-step synthesis, intrinsic photoluminescence property and intense chemiluminescence enhancement
Novel cookie-with-chocolate carbon dots displaying extremely acidophilic high luminescence
pH-dependent synthesis of novel structure-controllable polymercarbon nanodots with acidophilic luminescence and super carbon dots assembly for white light-emitting diodes
Graphene quantum dots, graphene oxide, carbon quantum dots and graphite nanocrystals in coals
One-step and high yield simultaneous preparation of single- and multi-layer graphene quantum dots from CX-72 carbon black
Multifunctional graphene quantum dots for simultaneous targeted cellular imaging and drug delivery
Novel and fast microwave-assisted synthesis of carbon quantum dots from raw cashew gum
Hydrothermal route for cutting graphene sheets into blue-luminescent graphene quantum dots
Electrochemical synthesis of photoluminescent carbon nanodots from glycine for highly sensitive detection of hemoglobin
Blue luminescent graphene quantum dots and graphene oxide prepared by tuning the carbonization degree of citric acid
Blue luminescent graphene quantum dots and graphene oxide prepared by tuning the carbonization degree of citric acid
Nitrogen-doped carbon dots derived from polyvinyl pyrrolidone and their multicolor cell imaging
Engineering surface states of carbon dots to achieve controllable luminescence for solid-luminescent composites and sensitive Be2+ detection
Colloidal carbon spheres and their core/shell structures with noble-metal nanoparticles
High pressure and temperature polymerization of amino acids suggesting a role of depth in the crust for chemical evolution
Synthesis and formation mechanistic investigation of nitrogen-doped carbon dots with high quantum yields and yellowish-green fluorescence
The photoluminescence mechanism in carbon dots (graphene quantum dots, carbon nanodots, and polymer dots): current state and future perspective
Thickness-dependent full-color emission tunability in a flexible carbon dot ionogel
Amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots: origin of tunable heterogeneous photoluminescence
